species.care_notes: Extremely drought tolerant. Let soil dry fully between waterings; rot is the main risk. Tolerates neglect.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry completely. Overwatering causes root rot.
Half-strength balanced fertilizer during growing season only.
Only when root-bound. Prefers being snug.

species.care_notes: Forgiving vining plant. Let top 1-2 inches dry out. Leaves curl when thirsty.

schedules.notes: Let top 1-2 inches dry. Curling leaves signal thirst.
Balanced liquid fertilizer during spring/summer.
Trim leggy vines to encourage bushier growth. Cuttings root in water.

species.soil_type: Chunky aroid mix (bark, perlite, coco coir)
species.care_notes: Wants a moss pole to climb. Fenestrations develop with maturity and good light.

schedules.notes: Let top 2 inches dry. Yellowing lower leaves = overwatering.
Heavy feeder in growing season. Balanced or high-nitrogen liquid fertilizer.
Wipe large leaves with damp cloth to remove dust and improve photosynthesis.
Repot when roots circle the bottom or emerge from drainage holes.

species.care_notes: Stores water in rhizomes; overwatering is the top killer. Thrives on neglect.

schedules.notes: Rhizomes store water. Err on the dry side; yellow stems = overwatering.
Very light feeder. Dilute balanced fertilizer a few times per growing season.
Slow grower; rarely needs repotting. Go up only one pot size.

species.care_notes: Dramatically droops when thirsty, recovers fast after watering. Likes consistent moisture.

schedules.notes: Likes consistent moisture. Dramatic droop = thirsty; recovers quickly.
Every 6-8 weeks during growing season. Excess fertilizer causes brown leaf tips.
Appreciates regular misting or a pebble tray for humidity.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe. Brown leaf tips usually mean fluoride/chloride in tap water; use filtered water.

schedules.notes: Let top inch dry. Use filtered water to avoid brown tips from chlorine.
Moderate feeder. Balanced liquid fertilizer in spring/summer.
Thick tuberous roots fill pots fast. Repot when roots push up through soil.

species.care_notes: Hates being moved and hates drafts. Wants bright consistent light and a steady watering rhythm.

schedules.notes: Consistent schedule is key. Let top inch dry; brown spots can mean overwatering.
High-nitrogen fertilizer during growing season for leaf production.
Dust large leaves with damp cloth. Helps light absorption.
Quarter-turn weekly for even growth. Hates full relocation but tolerates rotation.

species.care_notes: Succulent. Water deeply but infrequently; let soil dry fully. Wants direct sun.

schedules.notes: Soak and dry method. Water deeply, then let soil dry completely.
Very light feeder. Dilute succulent fertilizer a few times per year.
Repot when offshoots crowd the pot. Separate pups during repotting.

species.care_notes: Wipe leaves to keep them glossy and dust-free. Let top inch dry between waterings.

schedules.notes: Let top inch dry between waterings. Drooping leaves = thirsty.
Moderate-to-heavy feeder in spring/summer. Balanced liquid fertilizer.
Wipe glossy leaves regularly. Dust blocks light on these broad surfaces.
Quarter-turn regularly for even growth toward light.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe but fussy. Needs high humidity and distilled/filtered water; crispy edges signal low humidity or hard water.

schedules.notes: Keep soil lightly moist, never soggy. Use filtered or distilled water.
Light feeder. Dilute balanced fertilizer in growing season. Flush soil periodically.
Daily to every-other-day misting, or use a humidifier. Crispy edges = too dry.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe and an excellent air purifier. Humidity is more important than light â€” fronds brown and drop in dry air. Does not like cold drafts.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged. Finger-test daily in summer.
Mist daily or set on a pebble tray. Brown frond tips = too dry.
Dilute balanced liquid fertilizer in spring and summer only.
Repot in spring when roots fill the pot.

species.soil_type: Well-draining rich potting mix; add perlite for drainage
species.care_notes: Needs at least 4-6 hours of direct sun to thrive indoors. Large dramatic leaves split naturally â€” this is normal, not damage.

schedules.notes: Let top 2 inches dry between waterings. Reduce in winter.
Heavy feeder in summer. High-potassium fertilizer to support large leaves.
Wipe large leaves to keep them dust-free. Boosts photosynthesis.
Prefers being slightly root-bound. Repot only when roots escape drainage holes.

species.care_notes: One of the most tolerant houseplants. Darker-leaf varieties handle very low light; variegated pink/red varieties need more light for colour retention.

schedules.notes: Let top 2 inches dry. Very tolerant of under-watering; overwatering causes root rot.
Light feeder. Balanced liquid fertilizer 2-3 times during growing season.
Slow grower; repot only when root-bound.

species.care_notes: Long-lived succulent that can become a small tree. Needs direct sun for compact growth; leggy stems in low light. Water sparingly â€” far more tolerant of drought than overwatering.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry completely between waterings. Wrinkled leaves signal thirst.
Dilute succulent fertilizer a few times per growing season. Avoid in winter.
Pinch stem tips to encourage branching and bushy shape.
Very slow growing. Repot only when completely root-bound.

species.care_notes: One of the easiest houseplants. Vines trail or climb; give it a moss pole or let it cascade. Yellow leaves usually mean overwatering.

schedules.notes: Let top inch dry. Very forgiving â€” tolerates skipped waterings better than wet feet.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly in spring/summer.
Trim long vines to encourage bushier growth.

species.care_notes: Toxic to humans and pets â€” sap causes temporary loss of speech if ingested (hence 'dumb cane'). Wear gloves when pruning. Fast-growing and impressive in bright indirect light.

schedules.notes: Let top 2 inches dry. Drooping leaves can mean both over and under watering; check soil first.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly in growing season.
Wipe large leaves with damp cloth. Wear gloves â€” sap is an irritant.
Fast grower; repot annually in spring.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe and one of the best low-light tolerant palms. Grows slowly into an elegant tabletop or floor plant. Brown leaf tips are common â€” usually too-dry air or fluoride in tap water.

schedules.notes: Let top inch dry. Avoid waterlogging â€” palms are prone to root rot.
Dilute palm or balanced fertilizer in spring/summer. Over-fertilizing causes tip burn.
Mist fronds occasionally or use a humidifier to prevent brown tips.
Very slow grower; repot only when root-bound.

species.soil_type: Very fast-draining aroid mix or cactus mix
species.care_notes: Pet-safe slow-growing trailing vine. Blooms fragrant star-shaped clusters if given enough light and kept slightly root-bound. Never cut spent flower spurs â€” they rebloom.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry almost completely. Thick waxy leaves store water â€” very drought tolerant.
Phosphorus-rich fertilizer in spring encourages blooms.
Prefers being root-bound; repot reluctantly only when truly cramped.

traits.value: stem cutting in water or soil

species.care_notes: Pet-safe. Leaves fold upward at night (nyctinasty) like praying hands â€” normal behaviour. Use filtered or room-temperature water; cold tap water causes leaf curl and brown spots.

schedules.notes: Keep soil lightly moist. Use filtered water at room temperature.
High humidity is key. Mist regularly or use a pebble tray.
Dilute balanced fertilizer in growing season. Very sensitive to over-fertilizing.
Repot in spring when roots circle the drainage holes.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe and fun to propagate â€” freely produces 'pups' at the base. Rotate regularly for even pancake-shaped growth. Drooping leaves = thirsty; curling = too cold.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry out between waterings. Drooping means thirsty; act quickly.
Dilute balanced fertilizer monthly in spring and summer.
Rotate weekly for symmetrical growth â€” leaves reach strongly toward light.
Repot in spring; separate pups at repotting for easy propagation.

species.care_notes: Fast-growing trailing plant with dramatic purple and silver striped leaves. Colour is best in bright light â€” low light causes green fade. Pinch tips regularly to prevent legginess.

schedules.notes: Keep soil lightly moist. Tolerates drying out but colours look better with consistent moisture.
Balanced fertilizer monthly in growing season.
Pinch stem tips every few weeks to encourage dense, bushy growth.

species.soil_type: Very well-draining chunky aroid mix (bark, perlite, coco coir)
species.care_notes: Dramatic dark leaves with bold white veining. Goes dormant in winter â€” losing all leaves is normal if temperatures drop. High humidity is non-negotiable; spider mites appear quickly in dry conditions.

schedules.notes: Let top 2 inches dry. Very prone to root rot â€” excellent drainage essential.
Mist daily or use a humidifier. Low humidity invites spider mites.
Balanced fertilizer in growing season; stop completely in winter dormancy.
Repot in spring using fresh chunky mix.

species.soil_type: Coarse orchid bark (not potting soil â€” roots need airflow)
species.care_notes: Pet-safe and the easiest orchid to grow indoors. After blooms drop, cut the spike above a node to trigger a second bloom. Transparent pots help you see when roots are silver (dry) vs green (moist).

schedules.notes: Water when bark is nearly dry and roots are silvery. Soak bark and drain completely â€” never let roots sit in standing water.
Weakly weekly: dilute orchid fertilizer to 1/4 strength every 1-2 waterings.
Repot every 18-24 months or when bark breaks down. After blooming is the best time.

species.soil_type: Chunky well-draining aroid mix with orchid bark
species.care_notes: Produces glossy waxy spathes (often red or pink) year-round in good conditions. The more humidity it gets, the better it blooms. Brown leaf tips signal low humidity or fluoride in tap water.

schedules.notes: Let top inch dry. Susceptible to root rot; drainage is critical.
High humidity encourages continuous blooming. Mist or use a humidifier.
Phosphorus-rich fertilizer monthly promotes blooming.
Repot every 18-24 months. Roots like to mound above the soil surface â€” this is normal.

species.care_notes: A stunning trailing succulent. Pearls shrivel from underwatering and turn mushy from overwatering â€” the former is easily fixed, the latter usually fatal. Needs a draining pot with no reservoir.

schedules.notes: Water thoroughly only when soil is bone dry. Shrivelled pearls = thirsty; mushy pearls = overwatered.
Dilute succulent fertilizer a few times in spring and summer.
Repot if root-bound or when soil becomes heavily compacted.

species.care_notes: Striking sculptural form with thin cane-like trunks. Extremely tolerant of neglect and low humidity. Yellow lower leaves are normal shedding; brown tips signal fluoride sensitivity â€” use filtered water.

schedules.notes: Let top half of soil dry. Very tolerant of drought. Use filtered water to avoid tip burn.
Balanced fertilizer 2-3 times per growing season. Less is more.
Slow grower; repot only when severely root-bound.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe and virtually indestructible â€” tolerates deep shade, irregular watering, temperature extremes, and neglect. One of the best plants for dim offices or hallways with no windows.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry almost completely. Very drought tolerant; overwatering is the main risk.
Dilute balanced fertilizer 2-3 times per year. Very light feeder.
Extremely slow grower. Rarely needs repotting â€” can stay in the same pot for many years.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe low-light gem with vivid red or white veined leaves. Wilts dramatically when thirsty but recovers quickly when watered â€” great visual signal. Thrives in terrariums where humidity stays high.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Wilting means it needs water immediately â€” recovers fast.
High humidity is essential. Mist daily or keep in a terrarium.
Very dilute balanced fertilizer monthly in spring and summer.
Pinch growing tips regularly to prevent legginess.

species.care_notes: Annual herb that needs full sun (at least 6 hours). Pinch off the top two sets of leaves once the plant reaches reasonable height for higher-quality, bushier growth. Can be started indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost.

schedules.notes: Keep soil rich and consistently moist. Basil wilts quickly — do not let it dry out.
Light balanced liquid fertilizer every 3-4 weeks during the growing season.
Pinch the top two sets of leaves regularly and remove any flower buds to keep the plant producing.

traits.value: seed or stem cutting in water

species.care_notes: TOXIC to dogs and cats (allium family — can cause hemolytic anemia). One of the easiest herbs to grow. Harvest once the plant is about 1 foot tall; safe to cut up to half the plant at a time. Purple flowers are edible.

schedules.notes: Keep soil lightly moist. Chives tolerate some drought but produce better with consistent moisture.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during the growing season.
Snip leaves about 2 inches above the soil. Cut up to half the plant at a time; it regrows quickly.

species.soil_type: Rich, well-draining potting mix; deep container for taproot
species.care_notes: Pet-safe annual that grows 3-4 feet tall — it needs a deep container and a sheltered spot as it is sensitive to wind. Direct sow where it will stay; taproot does not transplant well. Seeds (coriander) are also a prized spice.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Drying out triggers premature bolting.
Light balanced fertilizer monthly during the growing season.
Harvest outer fronds regularly from the top. Once flower heads appear, harvest the whole plant — seeds are also valuable.

traits.value: seed — direct sow, does not transplant well

species.soil_type: Rich, moist potting mix — adaptable to most soils
species.care_notes: Toxic to cats; mildly so to dogs (essential oils). Hardy, quick-growing perennial — always keep in its own pot to contain spreading roots. Harvest in the morning when essential oils are strongest. Start indoors for controlled conditions.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Mint is thirstier than most herbs; check daily in warm weather.
Light feeding only — too much fertilizer reduces flavour intensity.
Harvest regularly from the tips to encourage dense, bushy growth. Dry harvested leaves in a cool, ventilated area.
Divide and repot annually — roots fill pots very fast.

species.soil_type: Rich, well-draining potting mix; deep pot for taproot
species.care_notes: Pet-safe biennial grown as an annual. Can be difficult to germinate — soak seeds overnight, changing water every 2 hours, to improve germination rates. Harvest outer stems first to keep the plant productive. May reseed itself if left to overwinter.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Parsley wilts quickly when dry and recovers slowly.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during the growing season.
Harvest outer stems at the base. Never take more than a third of the plant at once.

traits.value: seed — slow, 3-6 weeks to germinate; soak overnight first

species.soil_type: Well-drained potting mix; tolerates dry conditions
species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Perennial that prefers dry conditions — overwatering is the primary risk. Slow germinator (up to 6 weeks from seed). Harvest the biggest leaves first and prune the top leaves early to encourage a fuller, bushier plant.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry between waterings. Sage prefers dry conditions — root rot is more common than drought stress.
Minimal feeding; too much nitrogen reduces the aromatic oils that give sage its flavour.
Harvest the biggest leaves first. Prune the top leaves early in the season to encourage a fuller plant.
Repot in spring. Woody plants may need replacing every 3-4 years.

species.care_notes: Perennial herb beloved by cats (and harmless to them). Start seeds 4-8 weeks before the final frost date. Very easy to grow indoors with full sun; pinch back regularly to prevent legginess and delay flowering.

schedules.notes: Keep soil lightly moist. Tolerates some drought once established.
Light balanced fertilizer monthly during growing season.
Pinch stem tips regularly to keep growth bushy and delay flowering.

species.care_notes: Annual herb grown for its leaves (cilantro) and seeds (coriander). Prefers partial sun and cooler temperatures — plant 2 weeks before last frost and keep below 75°F to delay bolting. Succession sow every 2-3 weeks for a continuous harvest.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Drying out accelerates bolting.
Light balanced fertilizer every 3-4 weeks during the growing season.
Harvest outer leaves regularly. Once flower stalks appear, harvest everything and resow.

traits.value: seed — direct sow, does not transplant well

species.soil_type: Well-drained soil — add perlite or coarse sand for drainage
species.care_notes: Perennial Mediterranean herb that needs maximum sun and very low humidity. Mildly toxic to cats and dogs (essential oils). Takes up to 2 years to reach maturity. Needs excellent drainage — root rot in wet or humid conditions is the main killer indoors.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry completely between waterings. Overwatering is the primary cause of death.
Very light feeder; avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers which promote leafy growth over flowers.
Cut back by one-third after blooming. Never cut into bare woody stems — they won't regenerate.

species.care_notes: Tropical grass grown as an annual in most climates. Start indoors February-March in warm conditions. Toxic to cats and dogs (essential oils cause GI upset). Loves heat and humidity — keep away from cold drafts and dry air.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Does not tolerate drought well.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during the growing season.
Mist foliage regularly in dry indoor conditions; lemongrass prefers high humidity.

species.soil_type: Poor to average, well-draining mix — oregano thrives with less nutrition
species.care_notes: Mediterranean perennial that thrives in poor, well-drained soil with full sun. Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Days to maturity: 85. Drought stress actually concentrates the essential oils — flavour improves with slight neglect and leaner soil.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry between waterings. Very drought-tolerant; overwatering is the main risk.
Minimal feeding — excess fertilizer dilutes the essential oils that give oregano its flavour.
Harvest stem tips frequently to keep growth bushy. Cut back hard after flowering.

species.soil_type: Well-draining, slightly alkaline or neutral mix; prefers less-acidic soil
species.care_notes: Perennial Mediterranean herb that can reach 4-6 feet tall. Needs full sun and excellent drainage. Days to maturity: 85. Thrives in southern states; prefers dry conditions. The number one indoor killer is overwatering and poor airflow.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry almost completely between waterings. Root rot is far more common than drought stress.
Very light feeder. A little balanced fertilizer in spring; none in autumn or winter.
Harvest young stem tips. Never cut back into bare woody stems — they do not regenerate.

species.soil_type: Rich, well-draining potting mix; slightly acidic
species.care_notes: Pet-safe natural sweetener plant; the longest maturation time of common herbs at ~100 days. Start indoors February-March. Harvest leaves just before flowering for maximum sweetness. Perennial in warm climates; grow as annual elsewhere.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged. Does not tolerate drought or soggy conditions.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly. Avoid high-nitrogen formulas which reduce leaf sweetness.
Harvest leaf tips regularly. Pinch flower buds as soon as they form to keep leaves productive.

traits.value: seed (start February-March indoors) or stem cutting

species.care_notes: Compact perennial herb that works well in containers and indoor settings. Days to maturity: 80. Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Tolerates partial sun but flavour is more intense with more light. Compact grower — ideal for windowsill herb gardens.

schedules.notes: Let soil dry between waterings. Drought-tolerant; far more tolerant of underwatering than overwatering.
Minimal feeding; excess fertilizer dilutes essential oils and flavour.
Harvest stem tips regularly. Cut back by a third after flowering to keep growth compact.

species.care_notes: Heavy feeder that needs 10-12 weeks of indoor starting. Soak seeds for 24 hours before sowing to improve germination. Space 3-4 feet apart; rows 6 feet apart. Expected yield 30-40 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Artichokes need consistent moisture during bud development.
Apply organic liquid fertilizer once a month — artichokes are heavy feeders.

traits.value: seed (soak 24 hours first) or crown division

species.soil_type: Well-drained, fertile soil; roots planted 8-12 inches deep
species.care_notes: Spring perennial that takes approximately 3 years before initial harvest. Commonly grown from one-year-old crowns rather than seed. Space 12-18 inches apart. After spears become spindly, allow foliage to mature to feed the plants for next season. Berries are toxic to pets.

schedules.notes: Water consistently during the young plant stage. Established plants are more drought-tolerant.
Apply compost or mulch as annual top dressing. Avoid heavy nitrogen during harvest season.
Cut back fern-like foliage to the ground in late autumn after it turns yellow.

species.soil_type: Adaptable; slightly acidic pH 5.8-6.5 — beans fix their own nitrogen
species.care_notes: Warm-season annual available in bush and pole varieties. Days to maturity: 60-75. Requires at least 6-8 hours of full sun. Beans fix nitrogen and improve soil — avoid excess nitrogen fertilizer. Harvest when pods still snap before beans fill completely. Pick frequently to keep plants producing.

schedules.notes: About 1 inch of water per week. Avoid saturating foliage. Keep soil moist during blooming to prevent flower drop.
Apply general-purpose fertilizer (half cup per 10 feet of row) after flowering begins. Avoid high nitrogen.

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual. Direct sow as soon as spring soil is workable; succession plant every 2 weeks until 6 weeks before frost. Space seeds 2-4 inches apart. Yields approximately 40 lbs greens + 100 lbs roots per 100 feet of row.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Inconsistent watering causes woody, cracked roots.
Apply 1-2-2 fertilizer before planting. Side dress every 3-4 weeks.

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual. Start seeds 6 weeks before transplanting; plant outdoors as soon as soil can be worked. Fall crops can be direct seeded in early summer. Space 12-18 inches; rows 24-36 inches. Yields approximately 75 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Broccoli needs steady moisture for good head development.
Apply 8-16-16 fertilizer at 2-3 lbs per 100 sq ft two weeks before planting. No additional fertilizer needed.

traits.value: seed — start indoors 6 weeks before transplant

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual. Start seeds indoors 4-6 weeks before the ground becomes workable. Space 18-24 inches apart, rows 30-36 inches. Yields approximately 75 lbs per 100 feet. Long season crop — plan for 80-100+ days.

schedules.notes: Consistent moisture is critical for sprout development. Do not let soil dry out.
Apply 5-10-10 fertilizer at 3-5 lbs per 100 sq ft, 1-2 weeks before planting.

traits.value: seed — start indoors 4-6 weeks before planting

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual. Direct sow when soil can be worked, or start transplants 4-6 weeks earlier. Space 16-24 inches; rows 18-30 inches. Yields approximately 150 lbs per 100 feet of row.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Inconsistent watering causes heads to crack.
Apply 5-10-10 at 3-5 lbs per 100 sq ft, 1-2 weeks before planting.

species.soil_type: Well-drained, sandy loam, loose — rocks or compacted soil causes forked roots
species.care_notes: Cool-season annual. Direct sow as soon as spring soil is workable. Succession plant every 2 weeks for continuous harvests. Space seeds 2 inches; rows 18 inches. Can be left in ground up to a month after maturity. Potassium in fertilizer promotes solid, sweet carrots.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist — irregular watering causes cracked or forked roots.
Apply 1-2-2 fertilizer when sowing. Side dress when top foliage reaches 8 inches tall.

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual. Plant 8 weeks before the last frost; direct sow in mid-summer for fall crop. Space 15-18 inches; rows 24-36 inches. Yields approximately 90 lbs per 100 feet. One of the more demanding brassicas — consistent moisture and temperature are critical.

schedules.notes: Consistent, even moisture is essential. Stress from drought causes small, loose heads.
Apply 8-16-16 at 2-3 lbs per 100 sq ft two weeks before planting.

traits.value: seed — start 8 weeks before last frost

species.care_notes: Cool to warm season annual. Direct seed mid-spring through mid-summer. Minimum soil temperature 55°F for direct seeding. Space 4-8 inches; rows 12-24 inches. Harvest outer leaves to keep plant productive. Yields approximately 50 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Chard tolerates heat and light drought better than most leafy greens.
Mix 1-2-2 fertilizer into soil 1-2 weeks before planting. Reapply every 4 weeks.

species.care_notes: Cool to warm season annual. Direct sow as soon as spring soil is workable; plant every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvests until 6 weeks before first frost. Space 12 inches; rows 18-24 inches. Yields approximately 75 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Collards are fairly drought-tolerant but produce better with even moisture.
Apply 8-16-16 at 2-3 lbs per 100 sq ft, 1-2 weeks before planting.

species.care_notes: Warm-season annual. Minimum soil temperature 55°F (65°F for super-sweet varieties). Plant in blocks rather than single rows for wind pollination. Separate varieties by at least 500 feet to prevent cross-pollination. Succession plant for extended harvest.

schedules.notes: About 1 inch of water per week. Consistent moisture during silking and tasseling is critical for kernel development.
Apply nitrogen-rich 6-3-3 fertilizer at planting time to support rapid growth.

species.soil_type: Well-draining soil — tolerates poor soils; fixes nitrogen
species.care_notes: Warm-season annual legume that thrives in heat and tolerates drought. Treat seeds with Rhizobium bacteria inoculant before planting to maximize nitrogen fixation. Direct sow in spring and summer after soil warms. Plant in tilled, firmed ground.

schedules.notes: Drought-tolerant once established. Water regularly during flowering and pod set.
Minimal feeding — cowpeas fix their own nitrogen. Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers which reduce pod yield.

species.care_notes: Heavy-feeding warm-season annual. Direct sow after last frost; or start transplants 3-4 weeks before last frost. Slicing types: 12-24 inch spacing, rows 5-6 feet. Pickling types: 8-12 inches, rows 3-6 feet. Side dress when vines reach 2 feet. Yields approximately 125 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: About 1 inch of water per week. Consistent moisture prevents bitter fruit.
Apply 10-10-10 at planting. Side dress with the same when vines reach 2 feet in length.

species.care_notes: Warm-season annual that needs warm nights (above 50°F). Start seeds indoors 8-12 weeks before final frost; optimal germination at 85°F soil temperature. Space 18 inches; rows 36 inches. Harvest when skin is glossy and hard — harvesting regularly promotes more production. Yields approximately 150 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Inconsistent watering causes bitter fruit.
Sensitive to high nitrogen — use high phosphorus 5-10-10. Side dress when plants begin to flower.

traits.value: seed — start indoors 8-12 weeks before last frost

species.care_notes: TOXIC to dogs and cats (allium family — causes hemolytic anemia). Plant cloves in fall just before first frost for harvest the following summer; in zones 8-10 plant in early December. Soak cloves for 2 hours before planting. Cover soil with 6-8 inches of straw mulch. Plant flat-end down, pointy end up, 2 inches deep.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist through winter and spring growth. Reduce watering as leaves yellow before harvest.
Apply organic fertilizer after planting. Optional: add seaweed fertilizer to soaking water to prevent fungal disease.

species.care_notes: Warm-season annual vining plant. Sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F or warmer. Space seeds 18-24 inches; rows 4-6 feet. Yields approximately 125 lbs per 100 feet. Ornamental gourds are not edible but safe for most pets.

schedules.notes: About 1 inch of water per week. Water at the base to avoid fungal disease on leaves.
Apply 10-10-10 complete fertilizer at 2 lbs per 100 sq ft prior to planting.

species.care_notes: Cool to warm season annual. Sow directly when soil is workable or start transplants 2-3 weeks before outdoor planting. Succession plant every 2 weeks until 6 weeks before first fall frost. Space 12-18 inches; rows 18-24 inches. Flavor improves after light frost. Yields approximately 75 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Kale tolerates light drought but produces better with even moisture.
Use high phosphorus 8-16-16 at 2-3 lbs per 100 sq ft, two weeks before planting.

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual with a swollen stem that tastes like mild broccoli. Sow when soil is workable; start transplants 2 weeks prior. For fall crops, sow 2 months before the first frost. Space 12-18 inches; rows 18-24 inches. Yields approximately 75 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist for good bulb development. Dry spells cause woody, bitter stems.
Apply high phosphorus 8-16-16 at 2-3 lbs per 100 sq ft, two weeks before planting.

species.care_notes: TOXIC to dogs and cats (allium family). Cool-season biennial grown as annual. Direct seed in spring once soil is workable. Start transplants 4-5 weeks before outdoor planting. Space 4-6 inches; rows 18-24 inches. Transplant depth 4-6 inches to blanch stems white. Yields approximately 150 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Leeks need steady moisture for thick, tender stems.
Fertilize every 2 weeks until plants begin to bulb. Use ammonium sulfate (21-0-0) in alkaline soil or calcium nitrate (15-0-0) in acidic soil.

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual that bolts in heat. Sow as soon as soil is workable; start indoors 3 weeks before transplanting. For fall crops, sow 6-8 weeks before first frost. Space 8-10 inches; rows 12-18 inches. One of the easiest vegetables for indoor container growing.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Lettuce has shallow roots and dries out quickly — check daily in warm weather.
Apply 10-5-5 fertilizer 1-2 weeks before planting; side dress every 2-3 weeks. Use 2-3 lbs per 100 sq ft.

species.care_notes: Heavy-feeding warm-season annual vine. Sow 1-2 weeks after last frost when soil reaches 65°F. Start transplants 2 weeks before planting. Space 18-36 inches; rows 6 feet. Most varieties slip off the vine when ripe; honeydew types are cut when skin turns color. Yields approximately 100 fruits per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Regular moisture during vine growth; reduce watering as fruits ripen to concentrate sweetness.
Apply 10-20-10 at planting and again when fruits start to appear. Heavy feeders.

traits.value: direct seed after last frost or transplant

species.soil_type: Peat moss growing medium in flat containers with drainage holes
species.care_notes: Harvested 10-15 days after germination — the easiest and fastest indoor 'garden.' Keep out of direct sunlight to prevent wilting. Scatter seeds evenly over 1 inch of growing medium; press gently into soil. Use a plastic dome initially to maintain humidity. Harvest by cutting just above soil; wash thoroughly.

schedules.notes: Water 1-2 times daily with a spray bottle. Evenly moisten the top layer of soil — never let it dry out.
Harvest by cutting just above soil level when first true leaves appear (10-15 days after germination). Wash thoroughly.

traits.value: seed — scatter densely over growing medium

species.care_notes: Warm-season annual that loves heat. Direct sow or transplant when soil reaches 65°F. Start transplants 3-4 weeks before outdoor planting. Space 12-18 inches; rows 24-36 inches. Harvest pods when 3-4 inches long — they become tough and fibrous if left to mature. Yields approximately 175 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: About 1 inch of water per week. Drought-tolerant but produces better with consistent moisture.
Apply 5-10-10 at 3-5 lbs per 100 sq ft, 1-2 weeks before planting.

species.care_notes: TOXIC to dogs and cats (allium family — causes hemolytic anemia). Day-length dependent: short-day varieties (11-12 hours) best for southern states; long-day varieties (14-16 hours) for northern states. Start seeds indoors 3-4 weeks before soil can be worked. Space 3-6 inches; rows 12-18 inches. Yields approximately 100 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist through bulb development. Reduce watering as tops fall over at maturity.
Onions need lots of nitrogen — use 10-5-5 or similar. Apply every 3-4 weeks during growth.

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual. Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked in early spring. Space seeds 1-4 inches; rows 36 inches. Avoid excess nitrogen — it promotes lush foliage at the expense of pod production. Yields approximately 25 lbs per 100 feet. Provide support for climbing varieties.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Consistent watering during flowering is critical to prevent flower drop.
Small amount of 10-10-10 at planting only. Too much nitrogen causes excessive foliage with poor pod set.

traits.value: direct seed as soon as soil can be worked

species.care_notes: Warm-season annual. Start seeds indoors 8-10+ weeks before last frost — peppers germinate slowly. Night temperatures must stay above 60°F. Days to maturity: 60-80. Self-pollinating; separate hot and sweet varieties to minimize cross-pollination. Harvest by hand-clipping to avoid stem breakage. Bell peppers yield 6-8+ fruit per plant.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist — about 1 inch per week. Especially important when fruits are developing.
Switch to phosphorus/potassium-rich fertilizer as peppers develop. Avoid excess nitrogen which limits fruiting.

traits.value: seed — start indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost

species.soil_type: Well-draining, evenly moist soil — avoid waterlogged or compacted ground
species.care_notes: The green parts, sprouts, and unripe tubers contain solanine — toxic to dogs and cats. Plant seed potatoes in early spring when ground is workable. Space 8-12 inches; rows 18-24 inches. Cut larger seed potatoes into 1.5-2 inch pieces with at least one eye; allow cut surfaces to callous 1-2 days before planting. Expect a 1:8 ratio (1 seed potato → 8 at harvest).

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist but not wet. Waterlogged soil causes rot. Reduce watering as tops die back.
Apply a balanced fertilizer at planting. Hill soil around stems as plants grow.

species.care_notes: Warm-season annual vine. Plant when all frost danger passes and soil exceeds 70°F. Space 2-4 feet; rows 6-12 feet. Heavy feeder — apply 5-15-15 at 2 lbs per 100 sq ft. Avoid excess nitrogen which hurts yields. Late May to mid-June planting yields an early October harvest. Yields approximately 300 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: About 1 inch of water per week. Reduce watering as fruits cure on the vine.
Apply 5-15-15 at planting. Side dress when vines begin to run. Avoid high nitrogen.

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual — one of the fastest vegetables to harvest. Best grown in spring and fall when temperatures are cooler. Direct sow as soon as soil can be worked. Space 1-2 inches; rows 8-16 inches. Sow every 10 days for a continuous harvest. Yields approximately 150 lbs per 100 feet.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Irregular watering causes cracked, pithy roots.
Minimal fertilizer needed — apply low-nitrogen 5-10-10 at sowing time only.

traits.value: direct seed every 10 days for continuous harvest

species.soil_type: Well-drained, fertile, pH 7.0-7.5 — may need lime to raise pH
species.care_notes: Cool-season annual that bolts quickly in heat or long days. Sow as soon as soil is workable; plant every 10 days for continuous harvest. Pre-treat seeds: soak overnight, dry for a few days, then refrigerate for 5 days before sowing. At 40°F germination takes 30 days; at 75°F just 5 days.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Spinach has shallow roots and wilts quickly when dry.
Minimal feeding — apply balanced 5-5-5 at 2 lbs per 100 sq ft before sowing.

traits.value: direct seed — soak and pre-chill for best germination

species.soil_type: No soil needed — jar or tray sprouter with drainage
species.care_notes: Grow on a counter out of direct sunlight. Soak seeds 8-12 hours in warm water before sprouting. Drain and rinse with room-temperature water 2-3 times daily. Keep moist but never sitting in standing water. Good air circulation is essential — never lid a sprouting jar. Dry for 12 hours before refrigerating.

schedules.notes: Rinse 2-3 times daily with room-temperature water. Drain completely after each rinse.
Harvest when sprouts reach desired length (typically 3-7 days after soaking). Wash trays thoroughly between batches.

species.care_notes: Warm-season annual. Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F. Summer squash: 12-24 inches spacing, rows 4-6 feet. Winter squash: 18-24 inches, rows 6-9 feet. Both yield approximately 200 lbs per 100 feet. Start transplants 2 weeks before outdoor planting.

schedules.notes: About 1 inch of water per week. Water at the base to reduce fungal disease.
Apply complete 10-10-10 fertilizer at 2 lbs per 100 sq ft. Side dress when plants begin to vine.

species.care_notes: Leaves, stems and unripe fruit contain solanine — toxic to cats and dogs. Warm-season annual. Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Maintain 70-80°F soil temperature for seedlings. Transplant when soil reaches 60°F. Space 24 inches; rows 36 inches. Provide even moisture to prevent blossom end rot. Rotate crops annually.

schedules.notes: Water only when soil feels dry to touch for seedlings. Mature plants need even, consistent moisture to prevent blossom end rot.
Apply high-phosphorus 5-10-10 before transplanting. Fertilize again when first fruits appear and at first harvest.

traits.value: seed — start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost

species.care_notes: Cool-season annual best grown in spring and fall. Direct sow as soon as soil is workable. Space 2-4 inches; rows 12-18 inches. Valued for both the root and the greens. Yields approximately 100 lbs roots and 50 lbs greens per 100 feet from 600 seeds.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Drought causes small, bitter, woody roots.
Apply 5-10-10 at 2 lbs per 100 sq ft, 1 week before planting.

species.care_notes: Heavy-feeding warm-season annual vine. Sow after frost danger passes when soil reaches 65°F. Start transplants 2 weeks before outdoor planting. Space 18-36 inches; rows 6 feet. Seedless triploid varieties require pollination from diploid varieties. Heavy feeders — side dress 33-0-0 when vines begin running and again during fruit development.

schedules.notes: Regular watering during vine growth. Reduce as fruits ripen to concentrate sweetness and prevent splitting.
Apply 5-10-10 at 3 lbs per 100 sq ft at planting. Side dress 33-0-0 when vines run and during fruit development.

species.care_notes: Hardy perennial cane fruit in zones 4-9. Needs full sun and good air circulation to prevent disease. Pruning encourages lateral branching and increases cane strength. Water 1-2 inches weekly; increase to 4 inches during harvest season. Plant in rows with support.

schedules.notes: 1-2 inches of water weekly; 4 inches during harvest. Deep, infrequent watering encourages strong roots.
Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring before growth begins. Avoid high nitrogen after mid-summer.
Prune out floricanes (canes that fruited) after harvest. This encourages lateral branching for next season's yield.

species.soil_type: Moist, acidic pH 4.0-5.2, well-drained — acidic soil is essential
species.care_notes: Perennial shrub hardy in zones 3-9. Needs very acidic soil (pH 4.0-5.2) — this is the most common reason for failure. Benefits from cross-pollination with 2-3 different varieties planted together. Water 1 inch weekly; 4 inches during harvest.

schedules.notes: 1 inch of water weekly; 4 inches during harvest. Mulch heavily to retain moisture and maintain soil acidity.
Use acid-forming fertilizer (ammonium sulfate) in early spring and again 6 weeks later. Never use lime or wood ash.
Prune in late winter before growth begins. Remove old, unproductive canes to encourage new fruiting wood.

species.soil_type: Moist, acidic pH 4.0-5.0, well-drained — peat-based soil ideal
species.care_notes: Low-growing perennial groundcover in zones 3-9. Requires very acidic soil and consistent moisture. Avoid excess phosphorus in fertilizer applications. Requires a cold dormancy period (winter chill hours) to produce fruit reliably.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist — cranberries need even moisture year-round. No puddles or waterlogging.
Acid-forming fertilizer low in phosphorus. Excess phosphorus stunts fruit production.
Light pruning in spring to remove winter-damaged vines and encourage bushy growth.

species.care_notes: TOXIC to dogs — grapes and raisins can cause kidney failure even in small amounts. Perennial vine in zones 4-9 that requires a sturdy trellis. Water 1-2 inches weekly; 4 inches during harvest. Aggressive pruning is essential — remove up to 90% of last year's growth in late winter.

schedules.notes: 1-2 inches per week; 4 inches during harvest. Avoid wetting foliage to reduce fungal disease.
Balanced fertilizer in early spring. Avoid high nitrogen after fruit set — it promotes foliage over fruit.
Prune in late winter: remove up to 90% of last year's growth. This is the most critical care step for good yields.

species.care_notes: Perennial in zones 3-9. Needs 6-10 hours of daily sun. Remove first-year flowers to establish a strong root system before allowing fruiting. Water 1 inch weekly, distributed over multiple applications. Mulch around plants to retain moisture and prevent fruit rot.

schedules.notes: 1 inch of water per week distributed over several applications. Keep foliage dry to prevent fungal disease.
Balanced fertilizer in early spring. After fruiting, apply again to build plant strength for next season.
Remove runners (stolons) unless you want new plants. Remove old leaves and spent flower stems regularly.

species.care_notes: Toxic to pets. Annual with fluffy, long-lasting flowers in blue, pink, or white. Start indoors December-March. Germination: 7 days at 75-78°F soil temperature; 8-10 weeks to transplant. Prefers partial to full sun. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Wilting in midday heat is normal — water in the morning.
Balanced slow-release fertilizer monthly during the growing season.
Deadhead spent flowers to encourage continued blooming.

species.soil_type: Well-draining potting mix; tolerates poor soil
species.care_notes: Pet-safe annual with honey-scented flowers that attract beneficial insects. Start indoors December-March. Germination: 8 days at 78-82°F soil temperature; 10-12 weeks to transplant. Excellent edging or ground cover plant. Shear back by half after first flush to encourage reblooming.

schedules.notes: Water regularly but allow soil to dry slightly between waterings. Drought-tolerant once established.
Light balanced fertilizer monthly. Avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes foliage over flowers.
Shear back by half after first flowering to refresh growth and encourage a second flush.

species.soil_type: Well-draining potting mix; tolerates poor, dry soil
species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets. Dramatic annual grown for vivid red, yellow, and green foliage as much as for flowers. Start indoors December-March. Germination: 14 days at 68-70°F; 7-9 weeks to transplant. Heat-loving and drought-tolerant once established.

schedules.notes: Water moderately; very drought-tolerant. Overwatering causes floppy stems and dull color.
Light feeding only — too much nitrogen reduces the vivid foliage coloring.

traits.value: seed — direct sow after last frost or start indoors

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Annual with large, daisy-like flowers in a wide range of colors. Start indoors January-April. Germination: 8 days at 70-75°F; 8 weeks to transplant. Susceptible to aster wilt — rotate plantings yearly and avoid overwatering.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Avoid overhead watering to reduce disease risk.
Balanced fertilizer monthly during growing season.
Deadhead spent blooms to extend the flowering season.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe annual (also called cornflower) with vivid blue, pink, or white blooms. Start indoors February-March. Germination: 8 days at 68-70°F; 4-6 weeks to transplant. Can also be direct sown outdoors. Excellent cut flower. Deadhead regularly to extend blooming season.

schedules.notes: Water regularly but allow soil to dry between waterings. Very drought-tolerant once established.
Minimal feeding — thrives in average to poor soil. Excess fertilizer promotes foliage over flowers.
Deadhead spent flowers to encourage continuous blooming.

traits.value: seed — direct sow or start indoors February-March

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets. Annual with delicate white or pink flowers commonly used in bouquets. Start indoors February-March. Germination: 8 days at 68-72°F; 6-8 weeks to transplant. Prefers alkaline, well-drained soil. Succession sow every 2-3 weeks for continuous blooms.

schedules.notes: Allow soil to dry between waterings. Drought-tolerant; overwatering causes stem rot.
Light balanced fertilizer monthly. Thrives with minimal feeding.

traits.value: seed — direct sow or start indoors February-March

species.care_notes: Pet-safe warm-season annual with dramatic feathery or crested flower heads. Start indoors January-April. Germination: 10 days at 70-80°F; 6 weeks to transplant. Heat-loving — do not plant out until nights stay above 60°F. The flowers hold color even when dried.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Does not tolerate drought or waterlogging.
Balanced fertilizer monthly during the growing season.

species.soil_type: Well-draining, fertile potting mix; slightly acidic
species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Annual grown primarily for vivid, patterned foliage rather than flowers. Start indoors December-March. Germination: 10 days at 70-75°F; 6-9 weeks to transplant. Prefers partial to full shade indoors. Pinch flower buds to maintain foliage color and compact shape.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Wilts quickly when dry; does not recover well from severe drought.
Balanced fertilizer monthly during growing season.
Pinch flower buds as soon as they appear to keep foliage vivid and growth compact.
Mist leaves regularly in low-humidity indoor conditions.

traits.value: seed or stem cutting in water

species.soil_type: Well-draining, average to poor soil — excess fertility reduces flowers
species.care_notes: Pet-safe annual with feathery foliage and bright daisy-like flowers. Start indoors January-April. Germination: 5 days at 68-72°F; 6 weeks to transplant. Thrives with neglect — rich soil and excess water reduce flowering. One of the most drought-tolerant annuals.

schedules.notes: Very drought-tolerant once established. Water deeply but infrequently.
Minimal feeding — thrives in poor soil. Rich soil and high nitrogen produce tall, floppy plants with few flowers.
Deadhead spent flowers to prolong blooming through autumn.

traits.value: seed — direct sow after last frost or start indoors

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs (causes GI upset). Cool-season annual with classic white and yellow blooms. Start indoors February-March. Germination: 8 days at 70-75°F; 5-6 weeks to transplant. Prefers cool temperatures — tends to decline in summer heat. Excellent for spring containers.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Daisies wilt quickly in dry soil but also dislike waterlogging.
Balanced fertilizer monthly during active growth.
Deadhead spent flowers consistently to extend the blooming season.

species.soil_type: Well-draining potting mix; allow to dry between waterings
species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Popular container annual. Start indoors November-February. Germination: 4-10 days at 70-75°F; 14-16 weeks to transplant (one of the longest lead times). Soak seeds before sowing to improve germination. Pinch off the top of the plant once growing strongly for bushy, full growth.

schedules.notes: Allow soil to dry slightly between waterings. Overwatering causes root rot; underwatering causes leaf scorch.
Balanced fertilizer monthly. Reduce feeding in autumn and winter.
Pinch off growing tips when young for bushy growth. Deadhead spent flower heads regularly.

traits.value: stem cutting or seed (soak seeds first)

species.soil_type: Moist, well-draining potting mix; slightly acidic
species.care_notes: Pet-safe annual ideal for shady spots. Start indoors December-March. Germination: 15 days at 68-72°F; 10-12 weeks to transplant. Cover pots with plastic bags to maintain moisture during germination. Susceptible to fungal disease — avoid overhead watering. Needs grow lights or indirect window light indoors.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Impatiens wilt quickly but recover with prompt watering. Avoid wetting foliage.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during growing season.
Appreciates humidity — mist the air around the plant (not the leaves) in dry indoor conditions.

traits.value: seed or stem cutting in water

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets (can cause GI upset and skin irritation). Warm-season annual. Start indoors January-April. Germination: 7 days at 70-80°F; 6-9 weeks to transplant. Space dwarf varieties 6-8 inches; giants 12-24 inches. Add general purpose fertilizer once a month. Excellent pest-deterrent companion plant.

schedules.notes: Water deeply but allow soil to dry between waterings. Overwatering causes root rot and powdery mildew.
General purpose fertilizer once a month during the growing season.
Deadhead spent flowers regularly to extend blooming through the entire season.

species.care_notes: All parts — especially seeds — are toxic to pets and humans. Night-blooming tropical vine with large, fragrant white flowers. Start indoors February-March. Germination: 8 days at 70-75°F; 6-8 weeks to transplant. Needs a sturdy trellis. Blooms open in the evening and close by morning.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist during active growth. Drought-tolerant once established.
Light balanced fertilizer monthly. Avoid high nitrogen which promotes vine growth over flowers.

traits.value: seed — nick or soak seeds 24 hours before sowing

species.care_notes: Seeds are toxic to pets and humans (contain LSA compounds). Fast-growing annual vine. Start indoors February-March. Germination: 8 days at 70-75°F; 4-5 weeks to transplant. Nick or soak seeds 24 hours to improve germination. Needs a trellis. Thrives on neglect — rich soil and excess water reduce flowering.

schedules.notes: Water moderately; allow soil to dry between waterings. Very drought-tolerant once established.
Minimal feeding — excess fertilizer produces lush vines with few flowers.

traits.value: seed — nick seeds or soak 24 hours before sowing

species.soil_type: Poor to average, well-draining mix — rich soil reduces flowering
species.care_notes: Pet-safe annual with edible flowers and leaves (peppery flavor). Start indoors February-March. Germination: 8 days at 70-74°F; 6 weeks to transplant. Thrives in poor, dry soil — rich soil produces lush foliage and few flowers. Excellent companion plant that deters aphids.

schedules.notes: Water sparingly; allow soil to dry between waterings. Very drought-tolerant — overwatering reduces flowering.
Minimal or no feeding — thrives in poor soil. Fertilizing causes excessive foliage at the expense of flowers.

traits.value: seed — direct sow after last frost

species.soil_type: Well-draining, fertile, slightly acidic potting mix
species.care_notes: Pet-safe cool-season annual with familiar 'face' flowers. Start indoors November-February. Germination: 16 days at 55-65°F (one of the cooler germination temperatures); 6-10 weeks to transplant. Thrives in cool weather — declines in summer heat. Excellent for late-winter and early-spring containers.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Pansies prefer cool, moist soil; they dislike both drought and waterlogging.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during active growth.
Deadhead spent flowers to extend blooming. Cut back leggy plants by half to encourage fresh growth.

traits.value: seed — start indoors November-February at cool temperatures

species.care_notes: Pet-safe annual with prolific, trumpet-shaped blooms. Start indoors December-March. Germination: 10 days at 70-80°F; 10 weeks to transplant. Sow seeds on the surface — do not cover (needs light to germinate). Pinch tips to encourage branching. A mainstay of summer containers.

schedules.notes: Water regularly; allow soil surface to dry between waterings. Overwatering causes yellowing.
Heavy feeder — apply balanced water-soluble fertilizer every 2-3 weeks for best flowering.
Pinch tips when young. Cut back leggy stems by a third mid-season to renew flowering.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe warm-season annual with bold spikes of scarlet (or white, purple) flowers that attract hummingbirds. Start indoors December-March. Germination: 15 days at 75-78°F; 9 weeks to transplant. Deadhead spent spikes to encourage continuous flowering.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist but not waterlogged. Tolerates some drought once established.
Balanced fertilizer monthly during the growing season.
Deadhead spent flower spikes to encourage continuous blooming through summer.

species.soil_type: Well-draining, average to fertile potting mix
species.care_notes: Pet-safe annual that tracks the sun (heliotropism) when young. Start indoors February-March or direct sow. Germination: 6 days at 70-80°F; direct sow preferred as roots dislike disturbance. Space standard varieties 24 inches; dwarf varieties 12 inches. Deep roots make it somewhat drought-tolerant.

schedules.notes: Water deeply once or twice a week. Established plants are drought-tolerant. Reduce watering when heads form.
Balanced fertilizer monthly. Avoid high phosphorus which can limit growth.

traits.value: seed — direct sow preferred after last frost

species.soil_type: Well-draining, fertile, slightly alkaline potting mix
species.care_notes: ALL PARTS TOXIC — seeds especially. Cool-season annual climbing vine with intensely fragrant flowers. Start indoors February-March. Germination: 8 days at 70-75°F; 4-6 weeks to transplant. Needs a trellis. Prefers cool weather — declines in summer heat. Soak or nick seeds before sowing. Deadhead daily to prolong blooming.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Consistent moisture is essential — drought stress stops flowering immediately.
High-potash fertilizer every 2-3 weeks during flowering to sustain heavy bloom production.
Deadhead every 2-3 days without fail — a single seed pod forming will stop the whole plant flowering.

traits.value: seed — nick or soak 24 hours before sowing

species.care_notes: TOXIC to pets — contains alkaloids (vincristine, vinblastine) used in cancer treatment. Heat-loving annual with long-lasting flowers. Start indoors December-March. Germination: 15 days at 70-80°F; 12 weeks to transplant. Self-cleaning — no deadheading needed. Excellent drought-tolerant container plant.

schedules.notes: Water when soil surface dries out. Very drought-tolerant once established — overwatering is the main risk.
Balanced slow-release fertilizer monthly. Avoid excessive nitrogen.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe heat-loving annual that blooms prolifically. Start indoors January-April. Germination: 7 days at 68-72°F; 5-8 weeks to transplant. Excellent cut flower — the more you cut, the more it blooms. Deadhead spent flowers to extend the season. Water at the base to prevent powdery mildew.

schedules.notes: Water at the base — wet foliage causes powdery mildew. Allow soil surface to dry between waterings.
Balanced fertilizer monthly. Heavy bloomer — consistent feeding keeps it productive all season.
Cut or deadhead spent flowers regularly. Cutting stems for bouquets directly encourages more flower production.

traits.value: seed — start indoors January-April or direct sow after last frost

species.soil_type: Average, well-drained soil — tolerates poor soil
species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs. Hardy perennial with flat-topped flower clusters. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; also sow outdoors in early spring. Soil temp: 60°F. Height 24-36 inches; spread 18 inches. Extremely drought-tolerant once established. Deadhead to extend blooming.

schedules.notes: Very drought-tolerant once established. Overwatering or heavy, wet soil causes root rot and floppy growth.
Minimal feeding — thrives in poor soil. Too much fertilizer causes floppy, weak stems.
Deadhead spent flower clusters to extend blooming. Cut back to basal rosette in autumn.

species.soil_type: Average soil — very adaptable; tolerates clay and poor drainage
species.care_notes: Pet-safe hardy perennial (sometimes grown as annual/biennial). Direct sow anytime; no indoor start needed. Soil temp: 60°F. Height 24-36 inches; spread 18 inches. Very hardy — survives almost any soil condition. Deadhead spent blooms for continuous flowering; allow some to set seed for self-sowing.

schedules.notes: Drought-tolerant once established. Water during establishment; minimal supplemental water needed after first season.
Minimal feeding — thrives with little or no fertilizer in average soil.
Deadhead to extend blooming; leave some seed heads for birds and self-sowing.

species.soil_type: Loose, well-draining soil — tolerates sandy and poor soils
species.care_notes: Pet-safe perennial with vivid red and yellow daisy-like blooms. Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost; or direct sow 1-4 weeks after last frost. Soil temp: 70°F. Height 24-40 inches; spread 14-16 inches. Extremely drought-tolerant. Short-lived perennial — divide every 2-3 years or treat as annual.

schedules.notes: Very drought-tolerant once established. Overwatering and poor drainage are the main causes of decline.
Minimal feeding; excess fertilizer shortens the plant's life and reduces flowering.
Deadhead frequently to extend the long blooming season. Cut back in late autumn.

species.soil_type: Sandy, well-drained soil — tolerates poor, dry soils
species.care_notes: Toxic to pets and livestock (contains cardenolides). Native perennial milkweed critical for monarch butterfly habitat. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; or direct sow 1-4 weeks after. Soil temp: 70°F. Height 24-36 inches. Deep taproot — do not transplant once established. Drought-tolerant.

schedules.notes: Very drought-tolerant once established. Minimal supplemental watering needed after first season.
Minimal or no feeding — thrives in poor soil. Rich soil causes floppy growth.

traits.value: slow to moderate (slow first year, fast once established)
seed (cold stratify 30 days) or root cutting

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets. Low-growing evergreen perennial covered in white flowers in spring. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; direct sow in early spring. Soil temp: 65°F. Height 1-15 inches; spread 8-10 inches. Shear back by one-third after blooming to maintain compact form.

schedules.notes: Allow soil to dry between waterings. Drought-tolerant once established; susceptible to root rot in wet soil.
Light balanced fertilizer in spring before flowering.
Shear back by one-third immediately after flowering to keep the plant compact and encourage next year's bloom.

species.soil_type: Well-drained soil — tolerates clay, sandy, and poor soils
species.care_notes: Pet-safe native perennial and medicinal herb. Direct sow outdoors in early spring (no indoor start needed). Soil temp: 70°F. Spread seeds 12-18 inches apart. Height 24-42 inches; spread 12-18 inches. Hardy and self-sowing. Deadhead to extend blooming; leave some seed heads for birds in winter.

schedules.notes: Drought-tolerant once established. Minimal supplemental watering needed after first season.
Minimal feeding — thrives in average, well-drained soil without amendments.
Deadhead to extend blooming. Leave some seed heads for wildlife; cut back in late winter.

traits.value: seed (self-sows readily) or division every 3-4 years

species.soil_type: Moist, well-draining soil — tolerates sandy and average soils
species.care_notes: Pet-safe perennial with bright yellow, daisy-like flowers that bloom for months. Direct sow outdoors 1-4 weeks after last frost. Soil temp: 70°F. Height 18-48 inches; spread 6 inches. Drought-tolerant and low-maintenance once established. Self-sows freely.

schedules.notes: Drought-tolerant once established. Water regularly during the first season to establish roots.
Minimal feeding. Too much fertilizer causes floppy stems and reduced flowering.
Deadhead to keep plants tidy and encourage reblooming. Cut back to basal foliage in autumn.

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to cats and dogs (causes GI upset). Tender perennial grown as annual in most climates. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Soil temp: 65°F. Height 12-36 inches; spread 16-24 inches. Pinch growing tips when young for bushier plants. Lift and store tubers before first frost in cold climates.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Consistent moisture is essential — drought causes poor flowering and tuber stress.
High-potassium fertilizer every 3-4 weeks during flowering. Avoid high nitrogen which reduces blooms.
Pinch growing tips when young. Deadhead spent blooms and remove side shoots on tall varieties for bigger flowers.

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets (causes GI upset). Hardy perennial with classic white-and-yellow blooms. Sow outdoors in early spring. Soil temp: 60°F. Height 12-36 inches; spread 8-20 inches. Deadhead regularly. Divide clumps every 2-3 years to maintain vigor.

schedules.notes: Keep soil moist but not waterlogged. Drought-tolerant once established.
Balanced fertilizer in early spring. Avoid overfeeding which causes floppy stems.
Deadhead spent flowers to extend blooming. Cut back to basal rosette in late autumn.

species.soil_type: Average, well-draining potting mix; slightly alkaline
species.care_notes: TOXIC — all parts are poisonous to pets and humans. Dramatic tall perennial with striking blue, purple, or white flower spires. Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost; direct sow 1-4 weeks after last frost. Soil temp: 60°F. Height 12-48 inches; spread 18 inches. Needs staking. Cut back after first flowering for a second flush.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Delphiniums are thirsty plants — do not let them dry out during flowering.
High-potassium fertilizer every 3-4 weeks during growth. Avoid high nitrogen.
Cut flower stalks back to basal leaves after first flowering to encourage a second flush in autumn.

traits.value: seed or basal cutting in spring

species.soil_type: Rich, well-drained potting mix; neutral to slightly alkaline
species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets (causes GI upset and skin irritation). Perennial with spicy-scented, clustered flowers in a wide range of colors. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Soil temp: 70°F. Height 18-24 inches; spread 10-12 inches. Deadhead to extend blooming. Short-lived — treat as biennial in cold climates.

schedules.notes: Allow soil to dry slightly between waterings. Good drainage is essential — dianthus rots in wet conditions.
Balanced fertilizer monthly during flowering season.
Deadhead spent flower clusters regularly. Cut back by one-third after the main flush to encourage reblooming.

species.care_notes: HIGHLY TOXIC — all parts are dangerous to pets, livestock, and humans. Contains digitalis glycosides. Biennial or short-lived perennial with dramatic tall flower spires. Sow outdoors in early spring. Soil temp: 65°F. Height 24-60 inches; spread 24 inches. Slow germinators. Self-sows prolifically.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist, especially during flowering. Does not tolerate prolonged drought.
Balanced fertilizer in early spring. Minimal feeding during flowering.
Leave the main spike to set seed (for self-sowing), then remove. Side shoots may produce a second, smaller flush.

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets. True (hardy) geranium — not to be confused with annual Pelargonium. Perennial with magenta, pink, or white flowers and attractive foliage. Start indoors 6-10 weeks before last frost; direct sow 1-4 weeks after last frost. Soil temp: 70°F. Height 6-36 inches; spread 6-20 inches.

schedules.notes: Water regularly until established; then drought-tolerant. Well-draining soil is essential.
Balanced fertilizer in spring. Minimal feeding — thrives with little amendment.
Cut back the entire plant by half after the main flowering flush to encourage a second bloom and tidy growth.

traits.value: seed or division in spring or autumn

species.soil_type: Well-drained, rich potting mix; slightly acidic
species.care_notes: TOXIC to dogs and cats — causes vomiting, diarrhea, and lethargy. Shade-loving perennial grown primarily for dramatic foliage. Start indoors 6-10 weeks before last frost; direct sow in early spring. Soil temp: 70°F. Height and spread 24-40 inches. Prefers morning sun and afternoon shade indoors.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Hostas prefer consistent moisture but tolerate brief drought once established.
Balanced fertilizer in spring and again in early summer. Avoid feeding in late summer.
Cut back dead foliage in autumn after frost. Divide clumps every 3-5 years to maintain vigor.

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets. Tall biennial or short-lived perennial with cottage-garden charm. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Soil temp: 65°F. Height 48-72 inches; spread 18-24 inches. Needs staking. Susceptible to rust fungus — avoid overhead watering. Self-sows to perpetuate a colony.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Water at the base — wet foliage promotes rust fungus.
Balanced fertilizer in spring. Side dress with compost in summer.
Allow seed pods to mature and self-sow before removing spent stalks. Cut back to basal rosette after seed set.

species.care_notes: TOXIC — seeds and pods are especially dangerous to pets and livestock. Perennial with spectacular tall flower spires. Direct sow outdoors 2-4 weeks after last frost. Soil temp: 70°F. Height 24-36 inches; spread 12-14 inches. Dislikes transplanting — direct sow where it will grow. Fixes nitrogen in soil.

schedules.notes: Water regularly until established; then relatively drought-tolerant. Avoid waterlogging.
Minimal feeding — lupines fix nitrogen and thrive in average soil. Avoid high nitrogen.
Cut back spent flower spikes to encourage side shoots and possible second flush. Cut back fully in autumn.

traits.value: seed — soak or scarify before sowing; direct sow preferred

species.soil_type: Loose, well-draining soil — tolerates poor, sandy soils
species.care_notes: Pet-safe native perennial with tubular flowers attractive to hummingbirds. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; direct sow 2-6 weeks before last frost. Soil temp: 70°F. Height 12-36 inches; spread 18 inches. Drought-tolerant once established. Short-lived perennial — divide or let self-sow to maintain a colony.

schedules.notes: Drought-tolerant once established. Overwatering or heavy clay soil causes root rot.
Minimal feeding — thrives in poor to average soil. Rich soil causes floppy growth and reduces hardiness.
Cut back spent flower stalks to basal foliage. Leave some for self-sowing.

species.care_notes: Mildly toxic to pets. Perennial with fragrant flowers in summer. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; direct sow in early spring. Soil temp: 60°F. Height 6-18 inches; spread 8-10 inches. Susceptible to powdery mildew — improve air circulation and avoid overhead watering. Deadhead to extend blooming.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist. Water at the base to reduce powdery mildew. Consistent moisture is key.
Balanced fertilizer in spring and again at flowering time.
Deadhead spent flower clusters. Cut back to basal foliage after flowering. Divide clumps every 2-3 years.

species.soil_type: Any well-draining soil — tolerates poor, sandy soils
species.care_notes: TOXIC — seeds and sap are harmful to pets. Annual or perennial depending on species. Direct sow anytime — tolerates light frost. Soil temp: 60°F. Height 24-30 inches; spread 8-18 inches. Keep soil moist but not soggy. Germination 10-15 days. Space 6-8 inches. Self-sows reliably.

schedules.notes: Keep soil moist but not soggy. Drought-tolerant once established; overwatering causes root rot.
Minimal feeding — thrives in average to poor soil. Light balanced fertilizer in spring.

species.soil_type: Rich, moist, slightly acidic potting mix
species.care_notes: Toxic — causes vomiting, skin irritation, and difficulty breathing in pets. Cool-season perennial with jewel-colored spring flowers. Sow outdoors in early spring. Soil temp: 60°F. Height 8-15 inches; spread 12-18 inches. Prefers cool, moist conditions; declines in heat. Excellent for early-spring indoor containers.

schedules.notes: Keep soil consistently moist — primroses wilt quickly when dry and do not fully recover.
Balanced fertilizer monthly during flowering. Reduce after blooming.
Appreciates high humidity. Mist the air around the plant (not directly on flowers) in dry indoor conditions.

species.soil_type: Average soil — very adaptable; tolerates clay, sandy, and poor soils
species.care_notes: Pet-safe, very hardy perennial. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; direct sow anytime. Soil temp: 60°F. Height 24-36 inches; spread 12-24 inches. Very hardy — survives almost any soil condition. Deadhead to extend blooming; leave late-season seed heads for birds. Self-sows reliably.

schedules.notes: Drought-tolerant once established. Minimal watering needed after the first season.
Minimal or no feeding — thrives in average soil without amendments.
Deadhead to extend blooming. Leave late-season seed heads for overwintering birds.

species.soil_type: Average, well-draining soil — tolerates poor soils
species.care_notes: TOXIC — causes vomiting, liver damage, and can be fatal to pets and livestock. Invasive perennial that spreads aggressively. Sow outdoors in early spring. Soil temp: 65°F. Height 36-48 inches; spread 6 inches. Historically used as an insect repellent. Grow in a contained pot to prevent spreading.

schedules.notes: Very drought-tolerant once established. Minimal supplemental watering needed.
Minimal or no feeding — thrives without amendments.
Cut back aggressively to prevent self-sowing and contain spread. Remove flower heads before they set seed.

species.care_notes: Pet-safe perennial with edible flowers. Start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Soil temp: 65°F. Height 4-8 inches; spread 6 inches. Cool-season performer — blooms best in spring and autumn, declines in summer heat. Shear back in midsummer to promote a fresh autumn flush.

schedules.notes: Keep soil evenly moist. Violas prefer cool, moist conditions; they decline in heat and drought.
Balanced liquid fertilizer monthly during active growth.
Deadhead spent flowers. Shear back by half in midsummer to encourage fresh autumn growth and blooming.
